preamble:
# Pina the Turtle вҖ” How to Draw and Play with Python

## A manual for girls and boys aged 7-9 and their parents

*An illustrated journey of 42 chapters, built by synthesising the best teaching techniques from around the world: from Papert's Constructionism to Singapore's CPA, from the Finnish storytelling of Hello Ruby to the Argentine bug liturgy, from English PRIMM to South African tangible tiles.*

---

parts.title: Before we start

chapters.title: Who this book is for
chapters.content:
This manual is written for two people together: **a girl (or a boy) aged seven, eight or nine** who is curious about what's inside a computer, and **a parent-coach** who sits beside her. It doesn't matter if the parent has never written a line of code in their life: the manual is built specifically to be used in pairs, with instructions for both child and adult.

The journey is designed to be done **in small doses**: 15-20 minutes a day, two or three times a week, is more than enough. The Dutch CodeUur studies have shown that, for eight-year-olds, the **weekly ritual matters more than the length of a single session**. Half an hour on Tuesdays and Thursdays works better than two hours all on Sunday.

The manual is divided into ten parts for a total of forty-two chapters. The first parts are short and introductory; the central parts (shapes, variables, decisions, functions) are the heart of the book; the last parts open the door to mini-games, debugging and critical thinking.

> рҹҗў **Pina says:** it doesn't matter how fast you go. What matters is that you go in the right direction. Turtles know this well.
chapters.title_it: A chi ГЁ rivolto questo manuale
chapters.summary: A short letter explaining who the book is for: a child aged 7-9 and a parent-coach. No previous coding experience required.

chapters.title: The philosophy: four ideas for learning well
chapters.content:
This manual rests on four educational ideas proven all over the world. Knowing them helps the parent trust the method and not skip steps.

**1. Low floor, high ceiling, wide walls** вҖ” Mitchel Resnick (MIT Media Lab, Boston) says that a good learning environment has three qualities: a low floor (anyone can start in a few minutes), a high ceiling (you can go as far as building a video game), and wide walls (everyone can choose their favourite theme: horses, space, magic). In the manual you'll always find three thematic tracks for every project.

**2. Body-syntonic learning** вҖ” Seymour Papert, who invented the Logo Turtle in 1967 specifically to teach children, said that geometry is not learned by looking but by moving. **Before writing `forward(100)`, walk like a turtle on the living-room floor.** Every chapter in this manual will tell you how.

**3. CPA вҖ” Concrete, Pictorial, Abstract** вҖ” the Singapore school (the same one that taught you the times tables with the bar model) applies CPA to coding too. First you touch an object (cards, soft toys, steps on the floor), then you draw on squared paper, finally you write the code. Three times the same concept, three different representations.

**4. PRIMM вҖ” Predict, Run, Investigate, Modify, Make** вҖ” the English National Centre for Computing Education has codified a five-step sequence for every code example: **Predict** what it will do, **Run** it, **Investigate** line by line, **Modify** a small piece, **Make** your own version. We will use this sequence for almost every example.

> рҹ’Ў **Did you know:** Constructionism says we learn by building things **we can be proud of**. That's why every chapter ends with a drawing or a mini-game you can show to your grandparents.

To these four ideas we add **two narrative ingredients**: Linda Liukas's storytelling (Finland, *Hello Ruby*) вҖ” the computer is a character with feelings вҖ” and Carol Dweck's (Stanford) **growth mindset**: we praise the process, never the talent, and the error is a friend that brings information.

**The four skills of computational thinking** we will train, following the classification by **Jeannette Wing** (Carnegie Mellon University, the article *Computational Thinking* in Communications of the ACM, 2006), are:

| Skill | What it means | Example in the manual |
|---|---|---|
| **Decomposition** | Breaking a big problem into small pieces | To build a house, first the walls, then the roof, then the windows |
| **Pattern recognition** | Spotting what repeats | Four equal sides and four equal rotations = a loop |
| **Abstraction** | Giving a name to an idea and hiding its details | `def square(side):` lets you stop thinking about the 4 forwards |
| **Algorithm** | A precise series of steps that solves the problem | The sequence `forward; right; forward; right...` is an algorithm |

For curious parents: these four skills are not just "for programmers". They are the four muscles needed for mathematics, for science, for organising a birthday party, for writing an essay. We are teaching programming **to train the mind**, not just to write code.

**A note on timing.** At eight years old a child can concentrate on a demanding task for **15-25 minutes**. Beyond that, attention drops and content turns into frustration. The manual is structured to be used in **short sessions**: a chapter is 1-2 sessions. Take it slowly. Turtles don't run.
chapters.title_it: La filosofia: quattro idee per imparare bene
chapters.summary: The four ideas that drive this book: low floor / high ceiling / wide walls; body-syntonic learning; Concrete-Pictorial-Abstract; PRIMM.

chapters.title: The parent-coach: three golden rules
chapters.content:
Dear mum, dear dad: this manual is written so that you can learn together with your daughter, even if you have never programmed. To do well we only ask you three things.

**Rule 1 вҖ” Pair programming: Driver and Navigator.** A technique born in the Israeli classrooms of the Technion: two people, one computer, two distinct roles.

- The **Driver** has her hands on the keyboard. She writes.
- The **Navigator** reads out loud, points to the line, suggests, checks.
- Every five minutes вҖ” use the oven timer! вҖ” you swap.

The parent must resist the temptation to "do it instead of". Your turn at the keyboard lasts at most two minutes. You're there as a safety net, not as a bulldozer.

**Rule 2 вҖ” The three Magic Makers questions.** When the child gets stuck, in France (Claude Terosier's Magic Makers school) the adults never give the answer. They only ask three questions:

1. *What did you want to happen?*
2. *What is actually happening?*
3. *What difference do you see between the two?*

These three questions force the child to verbalise her thinking, and almost always the bug jumps out by itself.

**Rule 3 вҖ” The bug liturgy.** In the Argentine public schools (Iniciativa Pensar, Buenos Aires), every error follows five steps that we will use too:

```text
                    THE BUG LITURGY
   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
   в”Ӯ 1. Read the red error out loud               в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ 2. Point at the suspect line                 в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ 3. Explain to your soft toy what you wanted  в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ 4. Change ONE thing at a time                в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ 5. Celebrate when you find it!               в”Ӯ
   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** in the Brazilian ProgramaГӘ! schools every chapter ends with a "show-and-tell": the child tells another child what they did. You too, at the end of every chapter, ask the child to tell you what she learned. That's the secret.

**Things NEVER to do (the parent-coach anti-decalogue):**

1. **Never say "you're not good"**. It doesn't exist. There is "you can't do this **yet**".
2. **Never take the keyboard for more than two minutes** when the child is stuck. You ask the questions. She finds the answer.
3. **Never correct without explaining**. If you see a bug, point it out, don't fix it.
4. **Never get bored**. If you're bored, she sees it and becomes bored too. Find something that genuinely intrigues you.
5. **Never say "it's easy"**. It isn't. Saying it's easy makes the child feel stupid when she's been at it for ten minutes.
6. **Never compare with other children**. Never. Never. Never.
7. **Never do two chapters in one day**. Consolidation happens at night.
8. **Never skip the unplugged activities** "because she can count already". The body learns things the mind cannot.
9. **Never end angry**. If the session goes badly, laugh together, close everything, go get ice cream. You'll pick it up again.
10. **Never forget to celebrate**. Every finished chapter is a win. A star on the calendar. A drawing on the fridge. A round of applause.

**A word about screen time.** This manual is not "passive time in front of the TV". It is **productive, social, creative time**. The difference with watching cartoons is exactly the difference between reading a book and reading a road sign: the first creates, the second just passes. Don't feel guilty: you're doing the right thing. At the same time, the eye gets tired. Every 20 minutes, **20 seconds looking at something far away** (the 20-20-20 rule recommended by eye doctors). Pina will wait.
chapters.summary: Three golden rules for the parent-coach: pair programming, three magic questions, and the bug liturgy.

chapters.title: Setup: install Python and the editor
chapters.content:
To get started you need two things: **Python** (the language) and an **editor** (the notebook to write it in).

**Python.** Go to [python.org](https://www.python.org/downloads/) and download version 3.13 (or newer) for your operating system. During installation on Windows remember to **tick the "Add Python to PATH" box**: it will save you a heap of headaches. On Mac and Linux it's ready out of the box. The `turtle` module we'll use is already included: you don't need to install anything else.

**The editor.** We recommend two, in order of preference:

1. **Mu Editor** ([codewith.mu](https://codewith.mu/)). Written by a teacher, Nicholas Tollervey, specifically for children. It has one big "Run" button, the text is large and readable, errors are explained in plain English. One-click installation on Windows and Mac. **It is our first choice.**
2. **IDLE**, the editor that comes with Python. Ugly but it works everywhere. Fine as a fallback.

If you don't feel like installing anything, there's a **zero-install** alternative: go to [trinket.io/python](https://trinket.io/python) or [replit.com](https://replit.com/), open a new "Python with turtle" and write directly in the browser. Works on Chromebook and tablet too.

> вҡ пёҸ **Warning:** don't install Visual Studio Code, PyCharm or other professional editors yet. At eight, they're like a Formula 1 car for someone learning to ride a bike: too many buttons, too much light, too much fear.

> рҹҺҜ **Try right now:** open Mu, write a single line `print("hello")`, press Run. "hello" should appear at the bottom. You've made your first program.

**Illustrated mini-guide to Mu Editor.** When you open Mu for the first time:

```text
   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
   в”Ӯ  Mu Editor                                             в”Ӯ
   в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
   в”Ӯ  [Mode] [New] [Open] [Save] [Run] [Stop] [REPL] [...]  в”Ӯ  <- toolbar
   в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
   в”Ӯ                                                        в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ  1  from turtle import *                               в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ  2  forward(100)                                       в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ  3  done()                                             в”Ӯ  <- writing area
   в”Ӯ                                                        в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ                                                        в”Ӯ
   в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
   в”Ӯ  >>> messages appear here (errors, output)             в”Ӯ  <- console
   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

- **Mode** asks you in which mode to work: choose "Python 3".
- **New** opens a new empty file.
- **Save** saves. Give it a name with no spaces, ending in `.py` (e.g. `my_square.py`).
- **Run** is the magic button: it runs your program. **You'll use it all the time.**
- **Stop** interrupts if something goes into a loop.

**Save in the right folder.** Create a folder `coding-pina` inside Documents. Always save there. When you grow up, it will be your portfolio.

**Frequent parent questions.**

- *Can I use Mac/Windows/Linux?* вҖ” Yes, Mu works on all three.
- *Can I use it on a tablet?* вҖ” No, Mu is desktop only. For tablet use [trinket.io](https://trinket.io/python) or [replit.com](https://replit.com).
- *How much space does it take?* вҖ” Python + Mu = around 200 MB. A crumb.
- *Do I need internet?* вҖ” Only to install. After that, everything works offline.
- *And if it closes by accident?* вҖ” Not a big deal. Reopen Mu, reopen the file. All as before.

---

parts.intro: > Pina the Turtle lives on a paper island full of grid squares. Every day she wakes up, puts on her green cap, picks up her brush and waits for someone to tell her where to go. Today that someone is you.

parts.title: Meet Pina the Turtle

chapters.title: Meet Pina
chapters.content:
Pina is a young turtle, curious, she loves to paint but is a bit absent-minded. She has a green cap, a round shell with the (x, y) coordinates drawn on it like a mini-map, and she holds a paintbrush in her mouth which can be raised (pen up, doesn't draw) or lowered (pen down, draws). She walks only when we tell her to walk, turns only when we tell her to turn. She can neither read nor guess: she does exactly what you order her, letter by letter.

Pina lives on a screen called the **Turtle window**. At the centre of the window is the point (0, 0): it's her starting point, like the home square in a board game. From there, every step forward moves her in the direction she's looking. At the start she looks to the right.

```text
    +y
     в”Ӯ
     в”Ӯ       (Pina starts here, looking >)
     в”Ӯ
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ  +x
     в”Ӯ
     в”Ӯ
     в”Ӯ
    -y
```

> рҹҗў **Pina says:** if I get lost, just press the X on the window and reopen the program. I always return to the centre, looking right. I'm as predictable as a good friend.

**Pina's story (narrative vignette).** Pina was born in 1967 in a big glass building called MIT, in Boston. A man with a white beard named Seymour Papert thought that children learn better if they *move* things rather than just looking at them. He invented a little turtle-shaped robot, with a marker under its belly, that walked across the floor listening to children's commands. That robot was called Logo. A few years later, from Logo was born the **virtual Turtle**, that lives inside the screen. That's what today is called Pina, and she's ours. She's over fifty years old but still very young.

**What's on Pina's shell (her coordinates).** Every point on the window has two numbers: **x** (how far right or left) and **y** (how far up or down). The centre is (0, 0). From the centre, looking right, Pina has growing x. If she turns upwards, she has growing y. It works just like the squared paper you learned at school with the points of the Cartesian plane. It is **the same Cartesian plane** of RenГ© Descartes, the French philosopher of the seventeenth century. Yes, programming and philosophy are cousins.

**The three things Pina knows about herself at every moment:**

1. **Where I am**: the position `(x, y)` on the screen.
2. **Where I'm looking**: the direction (in degrees: 0 = right, 90 = up, 180 = left, 270 = down).
3. **Whether my pen is down**: that is, whether she's drawing or just walking.

Change one of these three things, and the effect on the screen changes. All of Pina boils down to these three things, and all of Python with Turtle boils down to learning to control them.
chapters.summary: Who Pina is, where she lives on the screen, and her three pieces of self-knowledge: position, heading, and pen state.

chapters.title: Your first program (PRIMM in action)
chapters.content:
Open Mu Editor. Click "New". Write exactly these three lines:

```python
# see examples/01_prima_riga.py
from turtle import *
forward(200)
done()
```

**Before pressing Run, play PRIMM.**

- **Predict (P):** take a piece of paper. Draw in pencil what you think Pina will do.
- **Run (R):** now press Run. What happened?
- **Investigate (I):** the first line `from turtle import *` tells Python "bring in the turtle's toolbox". The second line orders Pina to go forward 200 steps. The third line (`done()`) keeps the window open until you close it.
- **Modify (M):** change `200` to `50`. Press Run again. What changes?
- **Make:** modify the colour by adding `color("red")` before the `forward`. Pina turns red.

![Pina's first line](/assets/turtle/01_prima_riga.png)

> рҹ’Ў **Did you know:** the Python turtle is the granddaughter of the one invented in 1967 by Seymour Papert at MIT to teach children geometry. He had a real little robot that walked on the floor with a marker under its belly.

**Driver/Navigator dialogue вҖ” the first three steps.**

> **Sofia (Navigator):** "Mum, why is there an asterisk after *import*?"
> **Mum (Driver):** "The asterisk means 'bring in **everything**'. `from turtle import *` means: take all the tools from the turtle's box."
> **Sofia:** "And if I only wanted one tool?"
> **Mum:** "You'd write `from turtle import forward`. But for now our asterisk is fine: it means 'all I need today'."
> **Sofia:** "Can I press Run?"
> **Mum:** "Wait. PRIMM: first predict. What will Pina do?"
> **Sofia:** "She'll go forward 200. I mean... how much is 200? How many centimetres?"
> **Mum:** "They're pixels, not centimetres. Pixels = tiny dots on the screen. On a Mu screen, 200 pixels is about 5 centimetres. Let's see if you guessed right."

**Six little variants to try** (Raspberry Pi Foundation rule: after the first success, modify straight away):

1. Change `200` to `50`. What happens?
2. Change `200` to `400`. What happens?
3. Change `200` to `0`. What happens?
4. Add `color("red")` before the `forward`. What changes?
5. Add `pensize(10)` before the `forward`. What changes?
6. Add a second line `forward(100)`. What does Pina do?

> вҡ пёҸ **Warning:** if you write `forward (200)` with a space after `forward` and before the parenthesis, does Python get angry? **No**, it actually works. But by convention you write it without the space: `forward(200)`. Every programmer in the world writes it that way. Better to do as everyone does.
chapters.summary: Your very first three-line program вҖ” `forward(200)` вҖ” explored with the PRIMM cycle: Predict, Run, Investigate, Modify, Make.

chapters.title: PinaвҖҷs basic commands
chapters.content:
Pina understands six fundamental commands. Learn them by heart, like the six face cards of a deck.

| Command | What Pina does |
|---|---|
| `forward(n)` | Goes forward `n` steps |
| `backward(n)` | Goes back `n` steps |
| `right(d)` | Turns right by `d` degrees |
| `left(d)` | Turns left by `d` degrees |
| `penup()` | Lifts the pen (doesn't draw) |
| `pendown()` | Lowers the pen (draws again) |

> рҹҺҜ **Try right now:** write a program that draws the letter L: `forward(100); right(90); forward(50)`. Does it work? Now try to draw your own initial!

```python
# see examples/33_iniziale.py
from turtle import *
# Example: the letter S
penup(); goto(50, 100); pendown()
setheading(180)
forward(80); right(90); forward(40)
left(90); forward(80); right(90)
forward(40); left(90); forward(80)
done()
```

![Stylised initial](/assets/turtle/33_iniziale.png)
chapters.summary: The six commands you need to know by heart: forward, backward, right, left, penup, pendown.

chapters.title: Unplugged activity: become the turtle
chapters.content:
Before writing more code, **unplug from the computer**. This chapter takes a quarter of an hour and happens in the living room.

**Activity 1 вҖ” Walk like Pina (Papert, body-syntonic).** Put masking tape on the floor to form a big square one metre per side. The child is Pina. The parent gives commands out loud: *forward three steps, turn right, forward three steps, turn rightвҖҰ* After completing the square, swap roles.

**Activity 2 вҖ” Command cards (CS Unplugged, New Zealand + Tangible Africa).** Take ten slips of paper. On each write a command: `forward 100`, `right 90`, `left 45`, and so on. Lay the slips in a row on the table. One player "runs" the row by moving a toy turtle on squared paper. The other invents new rows.

**Activity 3 вҖ” The blindfolded robot (Code for Fun, Singapore).** Mum puts on a blindfold and becomes a robot. The child must guide her with the commands `forward`, `right`, `left` to make her fetch an object in another room. Only these commands: no "go a bit more to the right". Only degrees and precise steps.

```text
                  THE CPA TRIANGLE
                          
              CONCRETE  (the body)
                   в”Ӯ
                   в–ј
              PICTORIAL (the paper)
                   в”Ӯ
                   в–ј
              ABSTRACT  (the code)
```

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** in South Africa, the Tangible Africa project brought coding to more than 200,000 children without using a single computer for the first months. Just cardboard tiles on a table. By the time they reach the keyboard, the thinking is already there.

**Activity 4 вҖ” Tim Bell's binary cards (New Zealand, CS Unplugged).** Prepare five cards. On each, draw dots on the front: **1, 2, 4, 8, 16** dots respectively. Nothing on the back.

Lay them in a row from right to left in this order: 16, 8, 4, 2, 1.

```text
   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
   в”Ӯ 16 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ 8  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ 4  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ 2  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ 1  в”Ӯ
   в”ӮвҖўвҖўвҖўвҖўв”Ӯ  в”ӮвҖўвҖўвҖўвҖўв”Ӯ  в”ӮвҖўвҖўвҖў в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ вҖўвҖў в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ вҖў  в”Ӯ
   в”ӮвҖўвҖўвҖўвҖўв”Ӯ  в”ӮвҖўвҖўвҖўвҖўв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ
   в”ӮвҖўвҖўвҖўвҖўв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ
   в”ӮвҖўвҖўвҖўвҖўв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ
   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

Now turn cards face up or face down: face-up cards count, face-down ones don't.

- For 1 вҶ’ only the 1 card up.
- For 3 вҶ’ cards 1 and 2 up (1+2=3).
- For 13 вҶ’ cards 8, 4, 1 up (8+4+1=13).

This is **binary**: how computers represent every number. A card up = 1, a card down = 0. The number 13 in binary is **01101**.

Exercise: how do you represent your own age? And the current year? Later (an advanced chapter) we'll write a Python program that draws the binary representation of a number with coloured squares.

**Activity 5 вҖ” The sorting network (CS Unplugged).** With chalk or masking tape, draw on the floor a network like this:

```text
  A     B     C     D
  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”ҳ     в””в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”ҳ
     в”Ӯ           в”Ӯ
     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
           в”Ӯ
           в–ј
```

Six children, each with a number. Each walks along their line. When two meet at a comparison point, they look at each other: the smaller number continues to the left, the other to the right. At the end the numbers come out **sorted**. It's a **parallel sorting algorithm**, and none of the six knows the whole list: each only makes local comparisons. Genius.

When in Python you write `sorted([3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6])`, behind the scenes something like this is happening, in microscopic form, inside the chip.

---
chapters.summary: Five unplugged activities away from the computer: walk like Pina, command cards, the blindfolded robot, binary cards, the sorting net.

parts.intro: > On the kitchen table there is a big white sheet of paper. Pina arrives slowly, brush in mouth. She stops in the centre. She looks straight ahead. She waits. вҖңWhat should I do?вҖқ she asks silently. You have five minutes to tell her: the grandparents are coming to see the drawing.

parts.title: Shapes and drawings

chapters.title: The square by hand
chapters.content:
First of all, the square done **by hand**, line by line, no shortcuts. We want the child to **feel** the repetition before we hide it in a loop.

```python
# see examples/02_quadrato_manuale.py
from turtle import *
color("blue")
pensize(3)

forward(120)
right(90)
forward(120)
right(90)
forward(120)
right(90)
forward(120)
right(90)

done()
```

![Square written by hand](/assets/turtle/02_quadrato_manuale.png)

**ParentвҖ“child dialogue (Driver/Navigator):**

> **Mum:** "How many times did you write `forward(120)`?"
> **Sofia:** "Four."
> **Mum:** "And how many times `right(90)`?"
> **Sofia:** "Four."
> **Mum:** "Does it look right to you to write eight lines to make a square? What would a lazy turtle say?"
> **Sofia:** "She'd say: 'repeat four times!'"

Exactly. Pina is a lazy turtle. And in Python there's a command to be brilliantly lazy.
chapters.summary: Draw a square by writing each command line by line. Feel the repetition before we hide it in a loop.

chapters.title: Your first `for`: organised laziness
chapters.content:
```python
# see examples/03_quadrato_loop.py
from turtle import *
color("purple")
pensize(3)

for side in range(4):
    forward(120)
    right(90)

done()
```

Same square, **two lines instead of eight**. Magic? No, pattern recognition: the **first** of the four computational thinking skills by Jeannette Wing (Carnegie Mellon).

```text
   for side in range(4):    <-- "repeat 4 times"
       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ forward(120)   в”Ӯ   <-- these lines
       в”Ӯ right(90)      в”Ӯ   <-- run four times
       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ       (side is 0, then 1, then 2, then 3)
```

![Square built with a loop](/assets/turtle/03_quadrato_loop.png)

**Watch out for three Python details:**

1. The **colon `:`** at the end of the `for` line. Without it, Python doesn't understand. If you forget it, the bug liturgy comes on stage.
2. The **indentation**: the lines inside the loop are **indented** by four spaces (or one tab). That's how Python knows what to repeat and what not to.
3. `range(4)` means "a sequence of numbers: 0, 1, 2, 3". That's **four** numbers (Python counts from zero). The name `side` is just a label: you could call it `pippo` and it would still work.

> вҡ пёҸ **Warning:** in Python, capitals matter. `Forward(120)` doesn't work. `forward(120)` does. Pina is a cat: she only listens if you speak in the right voice.

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** in Japan, fifth-grade programming teaching explicitly includes drawing **regular polygons** with the turtle. It's the best way to link the geometry of the program with the geometry of the notebook.

**"Treat Hunt" exercise (Coding GoГ»ter, France).** Mum, take the program above and introduce **a deliberate bug**. Possible bugs:

- Change `range(4)` to `range(3)`.
- Change `right(90)` to `right(80)`.
- Remove the colon `:`.
- Remove the indentation.

Now ask the child to run the program. Goal: she must find the bug. When she finds it, there's a treat (a real, chocolate one!) as a prize. In French schools this technique turns debugging into a treasure hunt.

**Creative variant "Wide Walls" (Resnick).** Three themed tracks for the square loop:

| Theme | What you change |
|---|---|
| рҹҗҺ **Animals** | Draw a horse paddock (big square) + a gate (line) |
| рҹҡҖ **Space** | Draw a black square (space) + 4 small yellow squares (stars) at the edges |
| рҹ§ҡ **Fantasy** | Draw a big square as a magic mirror + a little star inside |

Choose your track and modify the program. Show the result to a grandparent.
chapters.summary: The `for` loop turns eight lines into two. The first pattern-recognition exercise in the book.

chapters.title: Triangle, pentagon, hexagon
chapters.content:
The loop becomes powerful when you understand a small piece of mathematics: **to close a regular polygon of N sides, the sum of all rotations must be 360В°**. So each rotation is `360 / N`.

| Shape | Number of sides | Rotation |
|---|---|---|
| Triangle | 3 | 120В° |
| Square | 4 | 90В° |
| Pentagon | 5 | 72В° |
| Hexagon | 6 | 60В° |
| Octagon | 8 | 45В° |

```python
# see examples/04_triangolo.py
from turtle import *
color("red")
pensize(3)

for side in range(3):
    forward(150)
    right(120)

done()
```

![Triangle](/assets/turtle/04_triangolo.png)

```python
# see examples/05_pentagono_esagono.py
from turtle import *
penup(); goto(-200, 0); pendown()
color("orange")
for side in range(5):
    forward(90)
    right(72)

penup(); goto(80, 0); pendown()
color("teal")
for side in range(6):
    forward(80)
    right(60)

done()
```

![Pentagon and hexagon side by side](/assets/turtle/05_pentagono_esagono.png)

> рҹҺҜ **Try right now:** draw a circle... using a loop! If you write `for i in range(360): forward(1); right(1)` you get almost a perfect circle. Why? Because 360 tiny sides look like a curve.

> рҹҸҶ **Big challenge:** draw a dodecagon (12 sides). How many degrees do you have to turn each time?

**Concept diagram: the exterior angle.** What you turn between one side and the next is the **exterior angle** of the polygon. It's not the interior angle! For example, in a square the interior angle is 90В°. The exterior angle is also 90В° (because 180 вҲ’ 90 = 90). Confusing? No: in an equilateral triangle, the interior angle is 60В°, but the exterior angle (what you turn) is 120В° (because 180 вҲ’ 60 = 120). For a regular polygon of N sides:

```text
   exterior_angle = 360 / N        <-- how much you turn between sides
   interior_angle = 180 - exterior_angle   <-- the "real" polygon angle
```

For the record, **the sum of all exterior angles of any convex polygon is 360В°**. It's a rule of Euclid. Every time you program a polygon, you're rehearsing a theorem of geometry.

**Pattern recognition (Wing).** Did you notice? For every polygon, the code is the same: only the number changes. The **pattern** is:

```python
for i in range(N):
    forward(SIDE)
    right(360 / N)
```

When you see a repeated pattern, programming asks you to make it a **function** (we'll do this in chapter 25). Keep this phrase in mind: *"Every time you feel yourself repeating, prepare a function."*
chapters.summary: Regular polygons: turn 360В°/N at each side. The geometric secret behind every shape Pina can draw.

chapters.title: The circle and the star
chapters.content:
For the circle Pina has her own command: `circle(radius)`.

```python
# see examples/07_cerchio.py
from turtle import *
color("magenta")
pensize(4)
circle(120)
done()
```

![Circle](/assets/turtle/07_cerchio.png)

And the star? There's a lovely little mathematical trick, known to teachers in Japan: to make a five-pointed star, just rotate by **144В°** (that is, `180 вҲ’ 36`) between one side and the next.

```python
# see examples/06_stella.py
from turtle import *
color("gold")
pensize(4)
begin_fill()
for point in range(5):
    forward(180)
    right(144)
end_fill()
done()
```

![Gold five-pointed star](/assets/turtle/06_stella.png)

The two new commands `begin_fill()` and `end_fill()` tell Pina: "whatever you draw between these, fill it with colour". It's like painting inside the lines after drawing the outline.

> рҹ’Ў **Did you know:** the star you just drew is called a **pentagram**. It was a sacred symbol for the Pythagoreans, a thousand years before Jesus. The geometry of code touches very ancient things.

**Why exactly 144В°?** The angle at the tip of a 5-point star is 36В°. The rotation between one tip and the next is 180В° вҲ’ 36В° = 144В°. Five rotations of 144В° make 720В° (that is, two full turns). That's why you draw the star **going around twice** around the hidden pentagon in the centre.

Try these variants, all of them "magic" stars:

| Star | Rotation | Number of points |
|---|---|---|
| 5-pointed star | 144В° | 5 |
| 7-pointed star | 154.28В° (or 102.86В°) | 7 |
| Star of David | (needs two overlapping triangles) | 6 |
| 10-pointed star | 144В° or 108В° | 10 |

> рҹҺҜ **Try right now:** modify the star code to use `range(7)` and `right(154.28)`. It's called an **heptagram** (7-pointed star). Mathematicians love it.
chapters.summary: The `circle` command and the magic 144В° that draws a five-pointed star (a pentagram).

chapters.title: Colours and thick pen
chapters.content:
Pina has a huge box of colours. She can use one for the **border** of the line (`color("blue")`), and another for the **fill** if you give two colours in a row (`color("navy", "skyblue")`).

```python
# see examples/09_quadrato_colorato.py
from turtle import *
pensize(4)
color("navy", "skyblue")
begin_fill()
for side in range(4):
    forward(150)
    right(90)
end_fill()
done()
```

![Filled blue square](/assets/turtle/09_quadrato_colorato.png)

To change the pen thickness: `pensize(5)`. To change Pina's speed: `speed(0)` (max speed, useful for long mandalas) up to `speed(10)` (slow).

```python
# see examples/08_colori_penna.py
from turtle import *
colours = ["red", "orange", "gold", "green", "blue", "purple"]
for c in colours:
    color(c)
    pensize(6)
    forward(120)
    backward(120)
    right(60)
done()
```

![Six-coloured ray star](/assets/turtle/08_colori_penna.png)

Now a cultural example: the **Italian flag**.

```python
# see examples/34_bandiera_italia.py
from turtle import *
def rectangle(x, y, w, h, colour):
    penup(); goto(x, y); setheading(0); pendown()
    color("black", colour)
    begin_fill()
    for i in range(2):
        forward(w); left(90); forward(h); left(90)
    end_fill()

rectangle(-150, -100, 100, 200, "green")
rectangle(-50, -100, 100, 200, "white")
rectangle(50, -100, 100, 200, "red")
done()
```

![Italian flag](/assets/turtle/34_bandiera_italia.png)

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** in Brazil, the BNCC ComputaГ§ГЈo programme requires every coding project to **celebrate diversity** by drawing flags, traditional patterns, country symbols. You can draw the coat of arms of your region!
chapters.summary: Border colour, fill colour, pen size and speed. Plus a culture moment: drawing the Italian flag.

chapters.title: Mandalas, snowflakes, rainbows
chapters.content:
Now that you know polygons and colours, you can take the leap: **nested loops**. That is, a loop inside another loop.

```python
# see examples/10_mandala.py
from turtle import *
speed(0)
pensize(2)
colours = ["red", "blue", "green", "gold", "purple", "orange"]
for turn in range(36):
    color(colours[turn % 6])
    circle(90)
    right(10)
done()
```

![Mandala of 36 coloured circles](/assets/turtle/10_mandala.png)

Thirty-six circles, each shifted by ten degrees. The result: a **mandala** that resembles those drawn in India, but also the rose windows of Italian churches, the patterns of Caltagirone ceramics, the mosaics of Pompeii. The mathematics is the same, the art changes.

```python
# see examples/11_spirale.py
from turtle import *
bgcolor("black")
color("cyan")
speed(0)
for step in range(120):
    forward(step * 2)
    right(91)
done()
```

![Magic spiral](/assets/turtle/11_spirale.png)

Did you notice **91** instead of 90? It's a clever trick: if you rotated by exactly 90 you'd just get a square that expands. By shifting **one degree more**, the spiral "slides" and creates a fantastic effect.

```python
# see examples/31_fiocco_neve.py
from turtle import *
bgcolor("midnightblue")
color("white")
pensize(2)
speed(0)

def branch():
    forward(80)
    for i in range(3):
        backward(20); right(45); forward(20)
        backward(20); left(90); forward(20)
        backward(20); right(45)
    backward(80)

for r in range(6):
    branch()
    right(60)
done()
```

![Six-pointed snowflake](/assets/turtle/31_fiocco_neve.png)

```python
# see examples/35_arcobaleno.py
from turtle import *
bgcolor("lightcyan")
pensize(20)
colours = ["red", "orange", "gold", "green", "blue", "indigo", "violet"]
penup(); goto(0, -200); setheading(0); pendown()
radius = 100
for c in colours:
    color(c)
    circle(radius, 180)
    penup(); forward(1); right(90); forward(20); left(90); pendown()
    radius += 20
done()
```

![Rainbow](/assets/turtle/35_arcobaleno.png)

A distant cultural example: the **Sankofa** symbol from Ghana, a circle with a little heart in the centre meaning "go back and get what you left behind".

```python
# see examples/36_adinkra_sankofa.py
from turtle import *
bgcolor("ivory")
color("darkred")
pensize(4)
penup(); goto(0, -80); setheading(0); pendown()
circle(80)
# ... see the file for the central heart
done()
```

![Sankofa symbol](/assets/turtle/36_adinkra_sankofa.png)

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** the American researcher Ron Eglash discovered that African fabric patterns and symbols contain **highly sophisticated fractals**, known to African artists centuries before Western mathematics gave them a name. Programming is also about learning to recognise mathematics across all cultures.

> рҹҸҶ **Big challenge:** create your personal mandala. Change the number `36` and the angle `10`. What happens if you use `60` and `6`? Or `72` and `5`? Discover the secret: the two numbers multiplied together must equal 360.

**The mathematical secret of the mandala.** To make a perfect mandala, you must satisfy two conditions:

1. **The total rotations must close the loop:** `number_of_iterations Г— angle = 360В°` (or a multiple).
2. **The "cyclic" colour must fit:** if you use 6 colours, you want 6 to divide nicely into the number of iterations.

```text
   EXAMPLES OF SUCCESSFUL MANDALAS:
   
   36 circles Г— 10В° = 360В°  вң“     (and 36 / 6 colours = 6 colour cycles)
   60 circles Г— 6В°  = 360В°  вң“
   72 circles Г— 5В°  = 360В°  вң“
   18 circles Г— 20В° = 360В°  вң“
   
   STRANGE MANDALA:
   30 circles Г— 11В° = 330В°  вң—  (a piece is missing!)
```

**Cultural history of the mandala.** The word "mandala" comes from Sanskrit and means "circle". In India and Tibet they are used for meditation: Buddhist monks draw them with coloured sand and **destroy them** at the end, to remember that everything changes. In Mesoamerica (Mexico, Guatemala) the Maya drew calendar-mandalas with 18 months and 365 days. In Italy, the **rose windows of Gothic cathedrals** (Santa Maria del Fiore in Florence, San Marco in Venice) are stone mandalas. And in Africa, the geometric patterns of the Kuba in Congo, or the Berber bracelets, follow the same radial mathematics.

When you write `for i in range(36):`, you're taking part in a tradition of **5000 years** of circular geometric art. Not bad.

---
chapters.summary: Nested loops produce mandalas, snowflakes, rainbows and Adinkra symbols. The mathematics of radial art.

parts.intro: > Pina wants to build a little house for her friend Hedgehog. Four walls, a pointed roof, a round window. вҖңSounds easyвҖқ thinks Pina. Then she looks again: each wall is a square, the roof is a triangle, the window is a circle. To build a house, you first need to know how to make shapes.

parts.title: Variables and creative freedom

chapters.title: The box with a label
chapters.content:
**A history of variables.** **Variables** are one of the most powerful concepts in modern algebra. They were used for the first time by FranГ§ois ViГЁte, a French mathematician of the sixteenth century, and refined by RenГ© Descartes in the seventeenth. Before them, mathematicians had to write in words "the number which, squared, makes 16". After them, you could write `xВІ = 16` and do mathematics. Variables are the great invention that allowed science to explode.

In programming, a variable is the same idea: a name that stands for **a value that can change**.

A **variable** is a box with a label written on top. Inside the box you can put a number, a word, a colour, a list. On the label you write whatever name you want.

```text
        в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
        в”Ӯ             в”Ӯ
        в”Ӯ     80      в”Ӯ  <-- contents: the number 80
        в”Ӯ             в”Ӯ
        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
              в–І
              в”Ӯ
           "side"    <-- label: the name of the variable
```

In Python you create it like this:

```python
side = 80
```

After this line, every time you write `side`, Python replaces it with `80`. Box magic!

```python
# see examples/12_variabile_eta.py
from turtle import *
color("crimson")
pensize(4)

side = 80
for i in range(4):
    forward(side)
    right(90)

penup(); goto(-200, 0); pendown()
color("teal")
side = 150          # change what's in the box
for i in range(4):
    forward(side)
    right(90)
done()
```

![Two squares of different sizes](/assets/turtle/12_variabile_eta.png)

Same code, two different squares, because we changed the **contents** of the box. This is the key to writing programs that adapt.

> рҹҺҜ **Try right now:** create a variable `favourite_colour = "magenta"` and use it in a loop to draw a whole flower in your favourite colour.

**Rules for variable names.**

- They must start with a **letter** (no numbers at the start).
- They can contain letters, numbers, and the underscore `_`.
- **They cannot contain spaces** (`my name` doesn't work, `my_name` does).
- No accents (`etГ ` doesn't work; `eta` does).
- No special Python words: `if`, `for`, `def`, `print`, `True`, `False`...

**Good naming habits.**

- **Descriptive** names: `square_side` is better than `sq` or `x1`.
- **Short** but clear names: `colour` is better than `pinas_pen_colour_at_this_moment`.
- Names in **Italian or English**, pick one and stick to it throughout the program.

```python
# Good
side = 80
number_of_petals = 8
favourite_colour = "pink"

# Bad
x = 80                   # What's x?
SquareSide = 80          # Python convention: lowercase, words_separated_by_underscore
ЕӮ = 80                   # Strange character, guaranteed error
```

**Types of variables.** In Python a variable can hold different things:

| Type | Example | What it holds |
|---|---|---|
| **int** (integer) | `age = 8` | A whole number |
| **float** | `pi = 3.14` | A number with a decimal point (in Python you use the point, not the comma!) |
| **str** (string) | `name = "Sofia"` | Text (always between quotes) |
| **bool** (boolean) | `is_summer = True` | True or false, only these two values |
| **list** | `colours = ["red", "blue"]` | A sequence of things |

> вҡ пёҸ **Warning:** in Python the decimal mark is the **point**, not the comma. `3.14` works; `3,14` is read as two separate numbers. An Italian-language detail that drives new programmers crazy.
chapters.summary: A variable is a box with a label. The greatest invention in all of mathematics, brought to your turtle.

chapters.title: Input: the first dialogue with Pina
chapters.content:
Up to now you've told Pina what to do. What if Pina asked you something? The command is called `input()`.

```python
# see examples/13_saluto.py
from turtle import *
hideturtle()
penup(); goto(0, 0)

name = input("What's your name? ")

color("purple")
write("Hello " + name + "!", align="center", font=("Arial", 32, "bold"))
done()
```

When you run it, **first** Mu asks a question in the bottom window: *What's your name?*. You answer *Sofia*, press Enter, and in the Turtle window "Hello Sofia!" appears in big letters.

![Personalised greeting](/assets/turtle/13_saluto_demo.png)

Input is a door between you and the program. It's what turns a drawing into an interactive experience.

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** in Argentina, the book "Doma de Serpientes para NiГұos" (the translation of Snake Wrangling for Kids) introduces `input()` already in chapter two. Because a program that talks is a program you can be friends with.

**Watch out: `input` always returns text.** Even if the person types `8`, Python gives you the **string** `"8"`, not the number. If you want to do maths with it, you must convert:

```python
answer = input("How old are you? ")
age = int(answer)           # converts the string to an integer
print("In 10 years you'll be", age + 10)
```

Without `int(...)`, writing `answer + 10` would give an error: *TypeError*. Pina would say: "You're trying to add text to a number. I can't."

**Interactive mini-project.** Combine `input`, `int`, and Turtle to make an on-demand polygon drawer:

```python
from turtle import *
n = int(input("How many sides do you want? "))
side = int(input("How long the sides? "))
for i in range(n):
    forward(side)
    right(360 / n)
done()
```

Four lines and you've built an **app** that draws any regular polygon. Show it to grandma. She'll love it.

> рҹҺҜ **Try right now:** ask the user their favourite colour, then draw 6 circles in that colour. Hint: after `input`, write `color(answer)`.
chapters.title_it: Input: il primo dialogo con Pina
chapters.summary: Pina speaks: the `input()` function lets you have a conversation. Type your name and Pina greets you.

chapters.title: Chance: random numbers
chapters.content:
To make every run **different**, there's a magic module called `random`. It works like this:

```python
import random
n = random.randint(1, 10)     # a random number between 1 and 10
c = random.choice(["red", "blue", "green"])   # a random colour
```

```python
# see examples/14_random_quadrati.py
import random
from turtle import *
speed(0)
colours = ["red", "blue", "green", "orange", "purple", "magenta", "teal"]

for q in range(15):
    penup()
    x = random.randint(-250, 200)
    y = random.randint(-200, 200)
    goto(x, y)
    pendown()
    color(random.choice(colours))
    side = random.randint(20, 80)
    for i in range(4):
        forward(side)
        right(90)
done()
```

![Fifteen random squares](/assets/turtle/14_random_quadrati.png)

Every time you run this program, the window is different. **Fifteen squares**, each time placed differently, coloured differently, sized differently. It looks like a Paul Klee painting.

> рҹ’Ў **Did you know:** in mathematics these are called "pseudo-random numbers" because the computer doesn't actually choose randomly: it follows a very, very complicated formula that *looks* random.

**The three `random` commands to learn.**

| Command | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `random.randint(a, b)` | An integer between `a` and `b` (inclusive) | `random.randint(1, 6)` вҶ’ simulates a dice |
| `random.choice(list)` | A random element from the list | `random.choice(["yes", "no", "maybe"])` |
| `random.shuffle(list)` | Shuffles a list | `random.shuffle(cards)` |

**Mini-projects with random:**

- **Dice roll**: `print(random.randint(1, 6))`. Run it 10 times. Different every time?
- **Odd or even**: ask if she wants odd or even, pick a random number, check.
- **Pina's answers**: a list of 10 phrases, prints one at random each time. It looks as if Pina is replying to you.

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** in Costa Rica and Brazil they teach `random` to simulate real things: rolling a dice, drawing a card, picking weather. It's called **modelling**. It's what scientists do to study earthquakes, epidemics, the climate.

**"PRIMM" exercise for random.** Open the file `examples/14_random_quadrati.py`. Run it five times in a row. Keep a notebook:

| Run | Largest square seen | Number of squares in the upper-right quarter |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ___ | ___ |
| 2 | ___ | ___ |
| 3 | ___ | ___ |
| 4 | ___ | ___ |
| 5 | ___ | ___ |

What do you notice? **No run is the same as another**. Yet the code is identical! This is the magical power of randomness.

> рҹҗў **Pina says:** randomness is the thing that lets even a small program become a piece of ever-new art. It's the secret of code artists: a few well-placed `random` calls, and every run becomes a unique drawing.
chapters.summary: Random numbers turn a copy into a surprise. Every run produces something new.

chapters.title: Teleport: `goto` and `setheading`
chapters.content:
Sometimes you don't want Pina to walk: you want her to **teleport** to a precise point. For that there are:

- `goto(x, y)` вҖ” Pina jumps to the point with coordinates `x, y`
- `setheading(d)` вҖ” Pina turns to look in direction `d` (0 = right, 90 = up, 180 = left, 270 = down)
- `penup()` before `goto` so the travel line isn't drawn
- `pendown()` afterwards to start drawing again

```python
# see examples/15_teletrasporto.py
from turtle import *
pensize(3)

penup(); goto(-200, 100); pendown()
color("red"); circle(30)

penup(); goto(0, -50); pendown()
color("blue"); circle(30)

penup(); goto(180, 80); pendown()
color("green"); circle(30)
done()
```

![Three teleported coloured circles](/assets/turtle/15_teletrasporto.png)

**Pina's direction: the four compass points.**

```text
                              90В°
                              (up)
                              в”Ӯ
                              в”Ӯ
        180В° в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ 0В°
        (left)                в”Ӯ              (right, default)
                              в”Ӯ
                              в”Ӯ
                             270В°
                             (down)
```

`setheading(0)` makes her look right, `setheading(90)` up, and so on. It's different from `right` and `left`, which turn *relative to the current direction*. `setheading` is **absolute**: it tells her exactly where to look.

**Coordinate systems.** On school squared paper, the Y axis goes down (the further down, the bigger Y). In Turtle it's the opposite: the Y axis goes up. To remember it: **Y as in "yes, up" вҖ” higher is better**. X goes right in both cases.

| Tool | X grows towards | Y grows towards |
|---|---|---|
| School squared paper | right | down |
| Turtle / mathematics | right | up |
| Screen (computer pixels) | right | down |

**Mini-exercise:** print Pina's current position with `print(pos())`. After every movement, print it again. You'll understand coordinates.
chapters.summary: Teleport Pina anywhere with `goto(x, y)` and tell her where to look with `setheading(angle)`.

chapters.title: Art project: PinaвҖҷs garden
chapters.content:
Now we put it all together. Variables + random + functions (to give an idea a name: "flower") = a unique garden every time.

```python
# see examples/16_giardino.py
import random
from turtle import *
bgcolor("lightyellow")
speed(0)

def flower(x, y, colour):
    penup(); goto(x, y); setheading(0); pendown()
    color(colour)
    for petal in range(8):
        circle(20); right(45)

colours = ["red", "magenta", "orange", "purple", "pink", "blue"]
for f in range(12):
    x = random.randint(-300, 250)
    y = random.randint(-200, 150)
    flower(x, y, random.choice(colours))

# The sun, top-left
penup(); goto(-280, 220); pendown()
color("orange", "yellow")
begin_fill(); circle(35); end_fill()
done()
```

![Pina's garden with flowers and sun](/assets/turtle/16_giardino.png)

Twelve flowers, position and colour random. Each run, a different garden. Print it and give it to grandma.

> рҹҸҶ **Big challenge:** add clouds in the upper part of the sky. Add green grass at the bottom. Add a butterfly crossing the scene.

---
chapters.summary: Your first big creative project: a whole garden of random flowers with stems, petals and grass.

parts.intro: > Pina has learned to draw squares big and small, but she has to rewrite the program every time. вҖңThere must be a smarter way,вҖқ she thinks. She sees her friend Mousy the programmer slipping a slip of paper into a shoebox. вҖңWhatвҖҷs that box?вҖқ asks Pina. вҖңItвҖҷs a variable,вҖқ smiles Mousy. вҖңItвҖҷs about to change your life.вҖқ

parts.title: Decisions: the turtle that thinks

chapters.title: `if`/`else`: the first decision
chapters.content:
**Thinking in decisions.** Up to now Pina has always done the same thing, predictably. But life is full of **decisions**: *if it's raining I take an umbrella, otherwise sunglasses*. Programming has the same tool: it's called `if`.

A decision is a **diamond** in flow diagrams (they draw them a lot in Hong Kong, before programming). Two arrows come out of a diamond: YES and NO. No other option: the computer is binary. It's either true or false. No middle ground.

**How to think about a condition.** A condition is always a **question** that can be answered yes or no. Examples:

- *Is the user older than 5?* вҶ’ `age > 5`
- *Is the number equal to 10?* вҶ’ `number == 10`
- *Is the colour "red"?* вҶ’ `colour == "red"`
- *Was it raining yesterday?* вҶ’ `was_raining_yesterday == True`

In Python, a **condition expression** is either `True` or `False`. These are the only two values Python allows: the **boolean**. The word comes from George Boole, an English mathematician of the nineteenth century who invented binary logic. Now you're using it.

A decision in Python has this form:

```python
if condition:
    what to do if the condition is true
else:
    what to do if the condition is false
```

```text
            в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
            в”Ӯ  Was it raining?в”Ӯ <-- the condition (diamond)
            в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                     в”Ӯ
              в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
              в”Ӯ             в”Ӯ
             YES           NO
              в”Ӯ             в”Ӯ
              в–ј             в–ј
       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ Umbrellaв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ Sunglassesв”Ӯ
       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

Example: red circles if small, blue if big.

```python
# see examples/17_if_else.py
import random
from turtle import *
speed(0)
pensize(2)

for c in range(20):
    penup()
    x = random.randint(-280, 250)
    y = random.randint(-200, 200)
    goto(x, y); pendown()
    radius = random.randint(10, 50)
    if radius < 30:
        color("red")
    else:
        color("blue")
    circle(radius)
done()
```

![Red and blue circles depending on size](/assets/turtle/17_if_else.png)
chapters.summary: The `if`/`else` keyword introduces choice. Pina branches like a path in a fairy-tale forest.

chapters.title: Comparing numbers
chapters.content:
To build conditions you need **comparisons**. There are six:

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `==` | equal to | `age == 8` |
| `!=` | different from | `colour != "red"` |
| `<` | less than | `points < 10` |
| `>` | greater than | `score > 6` |
| `<=` | less or equal | `sides <= 6` |
| `>=` | greater or equal | `time >= 0` |

> вҡ пёҸ **Warning:** `=` is assignment (puts something into the box), `==` is comparison (asks "is it equal?"). One of the classic traps! When you write `if`, you always need the double equal.

With `elif` (else if) you can chain several choices:

```python
# see examples/18_confronti.py
from turtle import *
hideturtle()
pensize(2)

scores = [3, 7, 5, 9, 2, 8]
x = -250

for p in scores:
    penup(); goto(x, -150); setheading(90); pendown()
    if p >= 7:
        color("green", "lightgreen")
    elif p >= 4:
        color("orange", "khaki")
    else:
        color("red", "salmon")
    begin_fill()
    for i in range(2):
        forward(p * 20); right(90); forward(40); right(90)
    end_fill()
    x += 70
done()
```

![Vertical bars coloured by value](/assets/turtle/18_confronti.png)

A **bar chart**: the height of each bar depends on the score, and the colour depends on the thresholds.

Here is a tiny interactive maths quiz that combines `input`, conversion to a number (`int(...)`), and `if`:

```python
# see examples/32_quiz_matematica.py
from turtle import *
hideturtle()
penup(); goto(0, 100)

a, b = 7, 5
write(f"What is {a} + {b}?", align="center", font=("Arial", 24, "bold"))
answer = input("Your answer: ")
goto(0, -20)
if answer.strip() == str(a + b):
    color("green")
    write("Well done!", align="center", font=("Arial", 28, "bold"))
else:
    color("red")
    write(f"It was {a+b}!", align="center", font=("Arial", 24, "bold"))
done()
```

![Maths quiz](/assets/turtle/32_quiz_matematica.png)

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** in Korean schools children do a very similar exercise in Year 2: 10 maths questions, each correct answer makes a character advance. Coding + maths revision together. Double benefit.
chapters.summary: Comparing numbers: greater than, less than, equal to. The grammar of decisions.

chapters.title: Events: keys and mouse clicks
chapters.content:
Up to now your programs ran and stopped. To make a **game** you need something more: the program must **wait** for you to press a key.

The three magic commands are:

- `screen.onkey(function, "Up")` вҖ” when you press the up arrow, `function` is called
- `screen.onclick(function)` вҖ” when you click the mouse, `function` is called (Python passes it the `x, y` coordinates)
- `screen.listen()` вҖ” tell the window: "pay attention to the keys!"

```python
# see examples/19_tasti_movimento.py
from turtle import *
screen = Screen()
shape("turtle")
pensize(3)
color("darkgreen")

def forward_step(): forward(20)
def backward_step(): backward(20)
def turn_left(): left(15)
def turn_right(): right(15)

screen.listen()
screen.onkey(forward_step, "Up")
screen.onkey(backward_step, "Down")
screen.onkey(turn_left, "Left")
screen.onkey(turn_right, "Right")
done()
```

Press the arrows and Pina moves at your command. You've just made your first **interactive** program.

![Arrow-key movement](/assets/turtle/19_tasti_movimento.png)
chapters.title_it: Eventi: tasti e clic del mouse
chapters.summary: Make Pina respond to key presses and mouse clicks. Welcome to interactive art.

chapters.title: Project: the magic whiteboard
chapters.content:
We combine `onclick` with `goto`: every time you click, Pina teleports there and draws a little star.

```python
# see examples/20_lavagna_magica.py
from turtle import *
screen = Screen()
bgcolor("black")
color("gold")
speed(0)

def star_at(x, y):
    penup(); goto(x, y); setheading(0); pendown()
    begin_fill()
    for p in range(5):
        forward(40); right(144)
    end_fill()

def on_click(x, y):
    star_at(x, y)

screen.onclick(on_click)
done()
```

![Magic whiteboard with gold stars](/assets/turtle/20_lavagna_magica.png)

> рҹҸҶ **Big challenge:** modify the program so that every click draws a **flower** instead of a star. Hint: copy the `flower` function from chapter 19's garden.

> рҹҗў **Pina says:** when you add an event, Israeli schools call this step "giving the program a soul". Before, the computer obeyed you. Now you talk back to each other.

**What changes with events.** Up to chapter 21 the programs were **linear**: one line after another, top to bottom. With events they become **reactive**: the program sits there, still, waiting for something to happen (a key, a click). When it happens, it jumps to a specific function.

```text
                LINEAR PROGRAM            REACTIVE PROGRAM
                
                в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                в”Ӯ line 1  в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ line 1  в”Ӯ
                в”Ӯ line 2  в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ line 2  в”Ӯ
                в”Ӯ line 3  в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ  WAITS  в”Ӯ <---- events
                в”Ӯ line 4  в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ  ev1?   в”Ӯ       fire
                в”Ӯ  END    в”Ӯ                в”Ӯ  ev2?   в”Ӯ       functions
                в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

**The `done()` or `mainloop()` at the end of the program is what says "keep listening".** Without it, the window would close at once and you wouldn't have time to press anything.

**List of recognised keys.** The main names `onkey` understands:

- `"Up"`, `"Down"`, `"Left"`, `"Right"` вҖ” the arrows
- `"space"` вҖ” the spacebar
- `"Return"` вҖ” the Enter key
- `"Escape"` вҖ” the Esc key
- `"a"`, `"b"`, `"c"`... вҖ” letters (lowercase!)
- `"1"`, `"2"`, `"3"`... вҖ” numbers

> рҹ’Ў **Did you know:** the first video game with events was *Pong*, made by Atari in 1972. One paddle, one ball. It was two lines of code in total. In chapter 31 we'll rebuild *Pong* with Pina.

---
chapters.summary: A full project: a magic whiteboard you draw on with mouse and keyboard. Save a screenshot when youвҖҷre done.

parts.intro: > Pina has learned to draw, but everything she does is always the same. Today she wants something different: she wants Pina to decide on her own вҖ” if itвҖҷs raining outside, draw a cloud; if itвҖҷs sunny, draw a sun.

parts.title: Functions: name your ideas

chapters.title: What is a function
chapters.content:
**Why functions exist? The story of Pina who draws five houses.** Imagine Pina has to draw five identical houses. If each house is 15 lines of code, the program becomes 75 lines, long and very boring. And if you realise the roof should be blue instead of red, you must modify 5 places in the code. A catastrophe.

The solution: write the 15 lines **once only**, call them `house()`, and then `house()` wherever you need. Five calls, 75 lines hidden. If you change the roof colour, you change it in **one place only**. Functions make programs **shorter, clearer, and easier to modify**.

> рҹ’Ў **Did you know:** functions are one of the oldest concepts in programming. They were invented in the 1950s by the pioneers Grace Hopper and John Backus. The word "subroutine" means "sub-routine", i.e. "a small program inside a program".

A **function** is a recipe. It has a name, a list of ingredients (the parameters), and a series of steps to execute. Once written, you call it by simply saying its name.

```text
        в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
        в”Ӯ  def grannys_cake(sugar,        в”Ӯ
        в”Ӯ                   flour):       в”Ӯ
        в”Ӯ      mix(sugar, flour)          в”Ӯ
        в”Ӯ      bake(180, 30)              в”Ӯ
        в”Ӯ      decorate()                 в”Ӯ
        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                       в”Ӯ
                       в–ј  later, when you want a cake:
                  grannys_cake(200, 300)
```

In Python a function is defined with `def`:

```python
def greeting():
    print("Hello Pina!")
```

To **call it** write `greeting()` (with the parentheses).

> рҹ’Ў **Did you know:** the word "function" comes from mathematics. A function eats a number and comes out with another number. In programming it eats parameters and comes out with an action or a result.
chapters.summary: A function gives a name to a recipe. The fourth thinking skill of computational thinking: abstraction.

chapters.title: Build a new command
chapters.content:
```python
# see examples/21_funzione_quadrato.py
from turtle import *
pensize(3)

def square():
    for i in range(4):
        forward(60)
        right(90)

color("red")
square()

penup(); goto(100, 0); pendown()
color("blue")
square()

penup(); goto(-150, 0); pendown()
color("green")
square()
done()
```

![Three identical squares in three positions and colours](/assets/turtle/21_funzione_quadrato.png)

You've just **invented a new command**: `square()`. From now on, every time you write it, Pina knows exactly what to do. You've extended Python's vocabulary.

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** in the Indian WhiteHat Jr schools the first function taught is precisely `square()`. Then `triangle()`. Then `house()` which combines them. It's the LEGO model of programming: small reusable pieces.
chapters.summary: Build your own command with `def`. You become the designer, not just the user.

chapters.title: Parameters: the changing function
chapters.content:
The problem with the `square()` function above is that it always makes a square of the same size (60). What if you wanted different squares without writing a thousand functions?

Answer: **parameters**. A variable that enters the function as an ingredient.

```python
# see examples/22_funzione_parametro.py
from turtle import *
pensize(3)
speed(3)

def square(side):
    for i in range(4):
        forward(side)
        right(90)

colours = ["red", "orange", "gold", "green", "blue", "purple"]
for n in range(6):
    color(colours[n])
    square(30 + n * 25)
done()
```

![Six concentric squares of growing size](/assets/turtle/22_funzione_parametro.png)

Every time you call `square(80)`, inside the function `side` is `80`. When you call `square(150)`, `side` is `150`. The function is the same, but the result changes.

Functions can **call other functions**:

```python
# see examples/23_petalo_fiore.py
from turtle import *
speed(0)
pensize(2)

def petal(colour):
    color(colour)
    for i in range(2):
        circle(80, 60)
        left(120)

def flower():
    petals = ["red", "orange", "gold", "green", "blue", "purple"]
    for c in petals:
        petal(c)
        left(60)

flower()
done()
```

![Flower with six coloured petals](/assets/turtle/23_petalo_fiore.png)

`flower` calls `petal` six times. **Decomposition**: the other great skill of computational thinking according to Wing. A big problem (the flower) is broken into small problems (the petals).
chapters.summary: Add parameters to your function. Now the same function can draw a thousand different things.

chapters.title: Project: house with a garden
chapters.content:
The do-it-all project of Part VI: a house with walls, roof, two windows and a tree next to it. Four functions, each with a clear task.

```python
# see examples/24_casetta.py
from turtle import *
bgcolor("lightblue")
speed(0)
pensize(2)

def go(x, y, direction=0):
    penup(); goto(x, y); setheading(direction); pendown()

def wall():
    color("saddlebrown", "burlywood")
    begin_fill()
    for i in range(4):
        forward(150); left(90)
    end_fill()

def roof():
    color("darkred", "red")
    begin_fill()
    for i in range(3):
        forward(150); left(120)
    end_fill()

def window():
    color("navy", "skyblue")
    begin_fill()
    for i in range(4):
        forward(40); left(90)
    end_fill()

def tree():
    color("saddlebrown")
    # ... (see full file)
    pass

go(-75, -75); wall()
go(-75, 75); roof()
go(-50, 0); window()
go(20, 0); window()
go(150, -75); tree()
done()
```

![House with roof, two windows and tree](/assets/turtle/24_casetta.png)

> рҹҸҶ **Big challenge:** add a **door** to the house. Add a sun. Add a dog in front of the door. Reuse functions you already have for wings and petals!

> рҹҗў **Pina says:** when you write `def`, you're building a little gift you can unwrap a thousand times. That's the magic of programming: you write one thing, you use it forever.

**The three golden rules of functions.**

1. **A function does one thing only.** If your function is called `draw_house_and_dog_and_sun`, it's too big. Split it into `house`, `dog`, `sun`.
2. **The name is a clear verb or noun.** `square`, `draw_flower`, `ask_name`. Never `foo`, `f1`, `thing`.
3. **If you find yourself copying code from one place to another, stop.** You probably need a function.

**Functions with a return value.** A function can also **return a result**, like a calculator. You do it with `return`:

```python
def double(x):
    return x * 2

result = double(7)   # result is 14
```

For now our `def`s don't return anything: they change the screen as a side effect. Later you'll discover that `return` is one of the most powerful concepts in programming.

**Concept diagram: how a parameter "travels".**

```text
   Above the function, you write:
   
      square(side=80)
              в–І
              в”Ӯ
              в”Ӯ Python copies 80
              в”Ӯ into the variable side
              в–ј
   Inside the function:
   
      def square(side):           <-- side is 80 here
          for i in range(4):
              forward(side)       <-- forward(80)
              right(90)
```

A function is like an apartment: it has its own "private" variables (the parameters), which don't interfere with those outside. It's a beautiful idea called **scope**.

> рҹҢҚ **From around the world:** in Dutch schools they teach the **apartment** metaphor: every function lives in an apartment. The variables inside the apartment are "yours". Those on the upstairs floors you can't see. When you return to the living room (the main program), the apartment's variables vanish.

---
chapters.summary: Big finishing project for Part VI: a complete house with garden, using functions you wrote yourself.
